Schismogenesis Gregory Bateson developed this concept (1958: 175) in his Cambridge doctoral dissertation, which broke both from structural functionalist and Marxist anthropology, earning the disapproval of Radcliffe-Brown and Malinowski. The word, just like ethos and eidos, central to Bateson’s book, is strongly Platonic in inspiration (for further discussion see Horvath and Thomassen, 2008). Bateson’s academic trajectory is in fact quite similar to that of Victor Turner. During his fieldwork among the Iatmul, Bateson became aware that the conceptual tools he had brought with him into the field were utterly inadequate. Bateson’s revolt was formulated in a most extraordinary piece of work, his 1936 Epilogue to Naven, the published version of his doctoral dissertation. This Epilogue combines, half a century before Bourdieu and the ‘reflexive turn’ in the social sciences, Liminality and the Modern 106 the conclusion of a piece of research with autobiographical reflections, creating – not surprisingly – a great deal of consternation. The Epilogue starts by Bateson revealing his complete failure to understand what was going on among the Iatmul, and the lack of any ‘guiding idea’ for his research (Bateson 1958 [1936]: 257–8). However, it soon becomes clear that Bateson’s ‘failure’ was not simply the result of his personal shortcomings as a researcher: it belonged to the ruling scientific paradigms. Bateson was astonishingly explicit here: ‘I could not (and still cannot) see that orthodox functional analysis was likely to lead anywhere’ (Bateson 1958: 257). Bateson was judged guilty of treason by his Cambridge environment and he would never get a job in an anthropology department. In Naven, Bateson makes a move similar to what Turner would later do: instead of attempting a ‘total’ or holistic description of an entire society, Bateson reads the Iatmul via a few central rituals. Closely observing the Naven ritual, characterized by very aggressive behaviour combined with mimicking, ridiculing and cross-dressing, Bateson came to the recognition that societies can be stuck for a long time in a state in which the previous unity was broken, and yet the schismatic components are forced to stay together, producing an unpleasant, violent, harrowing, truly miserable existence. Not surprisingly, Girard read Bateson’s work on the Iatmul with great interest, as it evidently confirmed some critical aspects of mimetic violence and imitation. However, in Naven Bateson’s aim was not simply to expose the flawed nature of functionalism or to denounce anthropological science as a hopeless ideal; far from it: he tried, hesitantly and in an honestly searching manner, to develop a different kind of conceptual vocabulary and epistemological footing for doing cultural analysis. It was as a part of this attempt that Bateson developed the concept of schismogenesis. In terms of definitions, Bateson (1958: 175) described schismogenesis as, ‘A process of differentiation in the norms of individual behavior resulting from cumulative interaction between individuals’. Just like van Gennep, Bateson sought to provide an empirically grounded analysis of differentiation processes, rather than assuming an original state of un-differentiation in ‘simple’ societies. While paying much attention to gender relations and ritualized mockery in his ethnographic analysis of the Iatmul, Bateson clearly indicated how schismogenetic processes could be at work in other spheres of social life. Right from the outset Bateson was moving the application of the concept beyond the specific cultural context. For Bateson schismogenesis could become part of any communication system or ‘communication relationship’ where individuals or groups interact. The behaviour of person X affects person Y, and the reaction of person Y to person X’s behaviour will then affect person X’s behaviour, which in turn will effect person Y, and so on, potentially leading to a ‘vicious circle’. Bateson’s schismogenesis theory is indeed about such circles. It is in the form of such circles that the ‘system’ is somehow ‘functioning’ although it may produce undesirable effects for everyone involved. That is why Bateson was not afraid to talk about pathologies in communication and in epistemology. Dimensions of Liminality 107 Bateson usefully distinguished between complementary and symmetrical schismogenesis. In the former, two ‘opposite’ types of behaviour reinforce each other in ‘opposite directions’: assertive versus submissive behaviour between two persons or two groups is the oft-quoted example here. In the latter, the ‘same’ behaviour will lead to more of the ‘same’ on the part of the other individual or group – a repetitive system of escalating competition: boasting leading to more boasting is the example invoked by Bateson himself (p. 177). Symmetrical relationships are those in which the two parties are equals, competitors, such as in sports, party politics or wars. Complementary relationships feature an unequal balance, such as dominance-submission (parent-child), or exhibitionism- spectatorship (performer-audience). Both types of schismogenesis can easily lead to extremities and become established as pathological states. There is every reason to bring together the insights of Gregory Bateson and Victor Turner and the comparative literature on the trickster. The process of schismogenesis can positively be produced by trickster-like figures. As Beebe expresses it, albeit in a different context, the trade of the trickster is to accomplish ‘an unsettling ambivalence, a splitting into two minds’ (1981: 38). This split, says Beebe, can occur ‘within a single individual, or between members of a large audience’ (ibid.). It is typical of the trickster, in art as in life ‘to split people into warring camps’ (ibid.). The trickster in many ways operates himself exactly the kind of double bind that Bateson so famously diagnosed as underlying schizophrenia. The victim of the double bind systematically receives contradictory injunctions or emotional messages on different levels of communication; he is caught in the communication game and cannot leave it. Drawing together the notions introduced so far, this means that schism and the splitting into two is a process that is particularly prone to unfolding in liminal moments, and that it can, under given circumstances, establish itself as a lasting form.
